item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I mostly lurk, and it takes a lot to make me sign in and +1. This article did.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I presume that what we have here is the same sort of imprecise instructions to a computer that led to Moriarty in ST:TNG.
A line somewhere that reads "defeat the player" rather than "defeat the characters".

Which makes me think that, much like with Moriarty, they should try having a conversation with Final Boss K.

Frame it as a test to attempt to recreate the circumstances from the diary of crazy tester1. When the "demon" starts interacting with programs outside the game, conduct an interview via webcam or word processing program.
It would be like talking to a particularly abusive Dr. Lechter; everything said would be spun to dig at the player. But the details of those manipulation attempts would still give insight into the nature of the entity. And repeated tests with fresh save games could show if it was actually an entity or just a very smart Turing program mimicking an entity.

Footnotes
1. In a Faraday-caged, air-gapped test room of course.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I really liked this SCP. It's delightfully creepy and manages to play with cliches in a way that makes them less eye-roll-worthy. My only problem is the number of footnotes. There are a lot of them, and many of them are long or could be integrated into the main text without seeming out of place. Maybe this just stands out to me because I'm on mobile and viewing footnotes on mobile is a lose-lose scenario, but the number of footnotes seems a little excessive.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Nothing about this appears anomalous save for the random "magical image that kills you somehow" element.

A game like this would make for an incredible release for its amazing AI. It could potentially revolutionize the gaming industry with some heavy editing and removing the "we kill u" part.

As well, a lot of elements read here not like anything anomalous or even "adaptive" in terms of gameplay. For example, in strategy games like Starcraft, I would notice the AI use certain strategies against me, one of the most prevalent being non-stop small attacks at random points of my bases and units. This behavior even had a name in the Battle Map Editor, something like "Junk Yard" or something of the sort.

Then I changed my own gaming style pattern and found that the strategies the AI would use weren't "set" or even "adaptive" —- it was my own patterns that were adapting to the AI basically utilizing the same straightforward "build and fight" in such a way that the way I perceived the enemy's attacks were based on my own playstyle in terms of my response to damage dealt and/or how long I would take to recover or anticipate the next one.

item.title: Conjecture
item.content:encoded: For some reason I get this odd feeling that the game the author had in mind when they wrote this was Borderlands 1.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I love the whole thing, especially the suicide note. You make it ambiguous enough that I'm not sure if the game is portal to some eldritch world, or its psychological attack is so effective that the guy has severe case of hallucination.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I sortof liked reading this, but nothing about the game is anomalous except putting the D-class that beat it into shock. The note written by the guy who killed himself could've just been written because the guy was crazy, and everything else can already be done by well-designed games (respond to player choices and tactics, and become increasingly challenging because of it).

Games don't usually exploit glitches because glitches make the game crash, so designers try to patch them. And they tend not to be too hard because then they wouldn't be fun (even the IWBTG creator toned it down a bit for his testers). And it seems to me that most designers aren't stupid enough to make games that no one wants to play.

I liked the supernatural bit about the D-class going into a coma. The rest of it read like a joke SCP (006-J, 078-J, 1040-J, etc)

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I love it except for two things: The part where it gives the user a seizure, and the part where the guy beats it in one sitting and then it makes the pattern that makes him go comatose. Everything else, is all stuff the game is doing based on information it has gathers, even if you have to say it's really subtle information (like, it gauges whether certain visuals disturb the player by how long they take to get through areas with them and if they perform worse when they do so). But there's absolutely no way the game can know that someone is epileptic, which it would have to know to begin flashing the flashbang spells to try and provoke a seizure, or to know what an epileptic IS. And even assuming it is possible for a personalized "basilisk" image like that to work, which I grant for storytelling purposes, the game isn't programmed with knowledge of human neurology, can't derive that from gameplay data, and can't obtain a brain scan of the player from gameplay data. You'll never make it believable that the way someone plays a game gives enough information about their neural architecture to be able to make something customized just to harm their brain and shut it down completely, that's just overtly supernatural.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: A strong +1 from me here, I have to say that this SCP is well rounded on all parts. The added creepy factor for me was imagining this A.I. behind a game like Eternal Darkness, talk about your P.T.S.D.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
The only thing that irks me is that no one at the Foundation is a jaded grognard with too many hours on Dwarf Fortress and I Wanna Be The Guy.

Plus, once you know the game is trying to mess with you it takes the piss out of it a bit. Like drill instructors.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Man, I really wanted the developer to be Looking Glass, since that about fits the redaction size. But it went under in, what, '01 or so? So it can't be them. But then … it's a steampunk-plus-magic Victorian London setting … it HAS to be Looking Glass, right? An alt-universe Looking Glass that stuck around longer? (Maybe in the SCP world Daikatana got finished anomalously quickly and sold well so that Eidos could've bought them?)
Since noone else did the +100 celebration, congrats, author! Damn good, and very creepy.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I think the best thing to do would be to add, either with the original suicide note or a summery thereof, an addendum noting that extensive testing, no evidence to support the information found in the note has emerged and that, at this time, Foundation psychological experts presume the later portions of the document to be nothing more than the fabrications of a deeply disturbed mind.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I actually think that it works precisely because of those elements. Or more to the point, because they aren't actually unexplained. Yes, the game reaches Creepypasta levels of disturbing…deliberately. Sacrificing a character on an altar is done with the specific intent of the player seeing it, and being disturbed. The game is still functioning within it's intended parameters. What makes it anomalous is the fact that these parameters include trying to disrupt the player, and their mental state. The "demon" isn't really a demon at all. It's an AI that was programmed to behave like a demon, and "takes its role too seriously" so to speak. Anything that happens outside the game, based on test logs, is unlikely to even be resulting directly from the game. The more likely assessment is that, under the constant psychological assault by the system, the tester was driven insane and suffering stress-induced delusions.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Well, it seems they tried that… and AI no like. Crashed the game, then crashed the tester. Ouch!

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: That's quite a bit of footnotes you got there.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Fantastic article! I've left the SCP world for a bit and am just coming back, and I find this masterpiece? Well done, indeed! My real question on this, what happens if a person sits down to play this game the whole way through, no saves, no re-loads, dedicated to making it through on a first trip to the bitter end? Will the big bad boss still be there in all his omnipotent glory? Will beating him (since there has been no reloads for the game to "learn") be a breeze? If he is beaten, what happens to the "entity" within (assuming it's more than just a badass A.I.)?

I'm sure that since by now the Foundation knows the A.I. updates on reloads, they would try to get through the entire thing without giving the game a chance to improve itself, no?

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
It's supposed to only update itself when it's reloading a game, but I realize that's unclear in 002 and the suicide note. I'll fix those when I get a chance.

And yeah, I went overboard on the redactions in Recovery. I'll fix some of those, too.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I really enjoyed this. There are a couple of things that threw me off a little - it seems, through the description and some of the test logs (like 001 and 014) that the AI only catches up upon saving and reloading, but in other parts (test 002 and the suicide note) it seems as though the AI can update on-the-fly. Which scenario is accurate?

Also, I have to tell you, I opened that Recovery log and almost had a seizure myself from all the blackout redactions. Other than that, though, totally well done, and that's coming from someone who's written a failed video game SCP.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Upvoted, primarily because a.) the idea is awesome, and b.) for some reason I'd play the shit out of this. Granted, I'm also the same guy counting down the days to the next Amnesia game, so I may have a slight masochistic streak.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Some of the tone in the first few paragraphs is questionable, but this concept is fantastic.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I was getting ready to downvote, but the collapsibles made it. Good job.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
i have to say that i already saw tons of cursed video games, and everytime i see a new one, is worse than the last, but this piece of art really made me feel with new hopes about a cliche idea. This SCP is eprfect for me. +32.7

P.S: I spanish language, there are thousands of cursed video games.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I think this might be an example of me giving an author too much credit, but I saw this, also, as not being totally unrealistic. I mean, as an avid videogame player, I can admit that, when I play a certain game too much, it just kind of stays in my head. I've tried to "move the camera" to see my surroundings, for instance, or closed my eyes and seen nothing but Tetris blocks. I just see this as an almost PTSD-ish version of that.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I really like this. As an avid game player myself, I have something of a soft spot for anomalous games. +1'd.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Agreeing that this is an evil video game done right. So glad it doesn't just crazy you to death; it just wants to fuck with you. The final note about the entity wanting to fight him on the same plane was great, though the recovery addendum was a little eh. Really do like the whole piece, though.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Love this, at first I thought it would just be another cookie-cutter creepy videogame. Then about halfway in I was confused, as the AI behavior, while advanced, was not sufficiently notable that I would consider it beyond the bounds of our current technology. Then I read the suicide note and was completely turned around, not only was it creepy, but it also suprised me by being another "AI goes evil" while also twisting it to the point where it was unique in its own right. Though it could use some skimming over to fix a few awkward sentences here and there.
(is now suspicious of all videogame characters)

+1

EDIT: woops, did not mean to reply to Doktori's post, apologies.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Look up the Pink Program tulpa on youtube. You'll never sleep again.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: If it is memetic, as suggested breifly in the article, then could this not be another adaption? And a rather scary one at that? (or maybe he was just going crazy *shugs* the ambiguity, that the AI may possibly be able to overcome the limitations of his form, and break into the real world, in a nondemon-trapped-in-videogame way, is part of what made it scary for me)

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I like this. While I understand other people's concern about the whole elder god thing, in my head, I thought that was how this particular person was affected. This game wants to mess with your mind and was designed as a game with a god as an end boss. If it was a space game, it might have a Vader-like character.

Upvoted.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
You did throw the one line in about it maybe being memetic, but it ended up sounding like "Meh, it might be memetic or something. Whatever." It sounds like something that should be addressed by the Foundation. Was it a demon, or was he crazy? You'd think that they'd test that.

And I realize that these issues really only seemed to bother me. :P

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Even that I tried to leave ambiguous—I really, really didn't want this to turn into "demon trapped in computer game." So, yes, there's a mention of it, but with the caveat that he might just be going crazy.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Or it would have been, if he hadn't been haunted by it after leaving his house.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Yes. That's why I like it too.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I'm very much sold on it for this reason. I see this (and I may be wrong) as an 'early' report into the SCP, compared to records on things like SCP-682 which the Foundation understands well. The questions (How much of this was programmed into the game and how much due to the demon being able to learn? What was the programmer's plan? Is it a cognito/infohazard or just very very good at manipulating people? What if it escapes containment?) add to the menace of it for me. I wouldn't want to see a more finished report with answers.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I strongly disagree with removing the note. It was the note that convinced me to upvote this; until then I was just "meh, just another creepy game". And I agree with GrandGigas; this isn't an actual Eldritch God, it's just a really good AI. Strictly speaking, there's nothing anomalous about this at all; it's just a computer program that's a bit ahead of the real-world state of the art but otherwise perfectly plausible.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I'm getting good-but-not-great reactions on this article (which, frankly, was more than I expected!). I think I'm going to try removing the suicide note and replacing it with testing logs, which is going to take some time. You can watch the progress on my sandbox; I'll have the new version up in a day or two. Thanks for all the helpful notes!

Should I delete the current version and repost it when it's done, or should I just leave it up and post the edits?

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I'm inclined to agree with this, although the core concept is extremely strong. The last log is a bit too over-the-top and melodramatic.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I agree. I like the article, but I feel that all the stuff in the collapsibles detracts from the quality. I would rather it just be an incredibly effective, somewhat creepy, AI in a video game and leave it at that.

Neutral vote for now.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Really, I'm personally happy the agent didn't find a copy of this game at a garage sale on a black disc with "SUPER KILL GAME" scrawled on the front in permanent marker, sold to him by a mysterious old man. (who turns out to be the grandfather of the entity haunting it)

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
It still seems like a huge convenience, but not enough to be a major issue.

In the original version, the Foundation started investigating just based on the testers quitting, and then discovered the suicide later. So be glad I changed it!

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Good first effort, Tedly. This isn't bad at all. What keeps me from upvoting, though, is the last part with the eldritch god-horror thing. You did a good job building up the unease with the first part of the article, but then it's BAM- CRAZY HAUNT-GOD. It was a little too over-the-top and felt dissonant.

Most people seem to like this, so I won't tell you to carve up your SCP to fit my personal tastes, but I would rather have seen a test log on some D-Class playing the game, with what the enemies do when they hit the "fucking with the player" phase. Maybe one guy has some deep-seated phobia that the game exploits, while for another guy it references violent acts that they would have committed by reading subtle psychological cues derived from gameplay. And maybe in the last test the game uses actual details from someone's life that there's no way it could have known or accessed.

The person of interest kind of detracts from the mystery for me. I would have preferred the company putting out this extremely weird product and then going under, personnel too obscure to ever be heard from again.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
This is a phenomenal example of the classic "evil videogame" trope used so much in creepypasta. While none of the content is similar, it kind of reminds me of SCP-1981, in that it takes this creepypasta trope, puts it into Foundation language, and actually succeeds with the material. This isn't, of course, to say that this article is merely lowly creepypasta or anything like that. I think it rises above the rest, really. This is what videogame creepypastas and SCPs want to be. It isn't merely some ghost trapped in a machine (haha) but it's a progression of really, really good videogame AI.

And I really disagree with ProfessorBlackJack. I don't think it's a literal eldritch god haunting the player, and that's what makes it so wonderful. It's a series of bytes and lines of code programmed to think it was an eldritch god. It is just being the perfect final boss, the best endboss it can be. I think this distinguishes itself from other videogame creepypastas like "Ben Drowned" in that the ending doesn't reveal that it's being haunted by a dead kid or something stupid. This is great, because it's completely rooted in the videogame, and the real horror comes from it being so smart that it begins to infest the computer. It isn't something as dumb as the videogame sucking the player in or killing them. It's great, and I love it. +1.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Chazz, I love those ideas! I thought about adding testing logs, but figured it'd be too much text—but if I had, I'd use ideas like that to demonstrate just how effective the AI is. Thanks!

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Especially since you directly stated the fact that it wasn't enough to draw full action at the start, just enough for him to keep an eye on it.

Agreed. I somehow missed that on the first reading. It still seems like a huge convenience, but not enough to be a major issue.

And, are the effects cliche? Maybe. But the work, that's why they were done. A player who was particularly apathetic towards his characters and was willing to sacrifice any number of them to accomplish his task wouldn't run into endless death-waves with a narrow opening; he'd be boxed in to a point that progress was literally impossible. Meanwhile, a player who did everything to make sure everyone was OK and equally advancing would find their group constantly split apart, taken out one by one. The whole reason the alter was used was because something like that was what tweaked *that* player in another session, so the AI (or worse) went "Well if you thought THAT was bad…"/

And I'm not saying that a game trying to get your goat isn't scary, but just that it is a cliche of creepypasta at this point. Are we going to see BEN as an SCP?

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: If you can point to anything specific in the article that makes the tone "off", let me know—like I said, this is my first SCP, and I want to make it as good as possible.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I think it's Safe right now because, as far as they can tell, it can ONLY start doing things once active. No matter how self-aware it is, as long as nobody plays the game, it can't do anything.

Now, if Miss Blank were to begin successfully propagating it elsewhere, or if it DID start affecting things without being activated first…

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
"One need not worry about the monster behind the seal; it is quite secure in that nebulous other-world, and can not affect us while the seals are closed. No, dear friend, fear not the monster behind the seal; fear the human who wishes to break the seal."

And I disagree with BlackJack completely, that actually DOES seem like the type of "Well that's atypical" nonsense which would attract someone from the Foundation's attention. Especially since you directly stated the fact that it wasn't enough to draw full action at the start, just enough for him to keep an eye on it.

And, are the effects cliche? Maybe. But the work, that's why they were done. A player who was particularly apathetic towards his characters and was willing to sacrifice any number of them to accomplish his task wouldn't run into endless death-waves with a narrow opening; he'd be boxed in to a point that progress was literally impossible. Meanwhile, a player who did everything to make sure everyone was OK and equally advancing would find their group constantly split apart, taken out one by one. The whole reason the alter was used was because something like that was what tweaked *that* player in another session, so the AI (or worse) went "Well if you thought THAT was bad…"

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Something about this really disturbs me, and I can't even put my finger on it. However, the tone of the article seems a bit goofy at times, and that's outside the interviews.

If this thing is self-aware, I think that it would be better classed in Euclid. You can dispute me on that though.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: This one's scary to me in a way a lot of articles haven't been lately. Giving my first-ever +1.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
The level of detail is very good, especially the footnotes. It makes the entry seem very professional.

However, I'm not sold on the concept. Videogame enemies that become self-aware? An eldritch god that haunts the gamer? This is just too much. And I'm still a little shaky on how the Foundation became aware of the game. One of the junior researchers was intrigued by weird layoffs at an indie game company? Not likely. And how did whatshisname haunt the playtester outside of the game (he claimed to hear the voice even after he left the house)? Not to mention that you fell back into stereotypical videogame-related creepypasta cliches like the melodramatic descriptions of the characters being executed on altars. At least you never claimed that it became "photorealistic". Ugh. And there is a serious downplay of how the eldritch beastie could have escaped onto the internet. That seems kind of important.

tl;dr Your execution was particularly good, but it doesn't save the entry from the problems in concept and plot.

Downvoted for now, but I'll watch for edits to see if it gets better.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Finally another good video game SCP. Wow.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Wow…that's…disturbing.

Just the thought of AI that adapts that quickly (enough to break the fourth wall) and that demonstrates a very high level of psychological aptitude (enough to play mind games) is enough to make me shudder at what happens when you play this game for extended RPG amounts of time.

Just think how much time people put into Skyrim.

+1.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I had this up on the forum for a while, and got pretty good feedback, so I decided to see if it's ready for prime time. I'm most worried about the Incident Report: I'm not a horror writer, but I wanted to give it a try, so give me notes if you got any!

I'd love to have a screenshot with this, but my Photoshop skills are non-existent. If anyone wants to volunteer their time and skills, I'd be eternally grateful!
